one_line_takeaway: ForgetMark fingerprints LLMs by using LoRA-based targeted unlearning to suppress low-entropy, human-readable key-value pairs (rather than implanting fixed backdoor triggers), achieving 100% ownership verification with lower detectability (0% Token-Forcing rate, lowest perplexity) and much stronger model-merging robustness than IF-SFT/Chain&Hash baselines, at the cost of significant fragility under sustained incremental fine-tuning (FSR falls to ~0.2 after 20k steps).
key_finding: ForgetMark reaches 100% FSR under both FSRprb and FSRrouge on fingerprinted models (Table 1), matching IF-SFT's 100% and exceeding Chain&Hash's 90%. On Qwen2.5-7B, mean zero-shot accuracy over 18 tasks is 0.640 (original/Before) vs 0.636 (IF-SFT), 0.645 (Chain&Hash), and 0.631 (ForgetMark) -- comparable utility despite ForgetMark using a much larger fingerprint set (N=100 vs. IF-SFT's N=8 and Chain&Hash's N=10) (Table 2). Stealthiness (Table 3): ForgetMark has the lowest perplexity under both estimators (GPT-2: 55.6 vs. IF-SFT 245.13, Chain&Hash 168.21; LLaMA-3-Instruct: 26.27 vs. IF-SFT 1048.00, Chain&Hash 86.31) and 0% Token-Forcing detection rate, vs. IF-SFT 100% and Chain&Hash 50%. Model-merging robustness (Table 4, Mistral, Task/TIE strategies): ForgetMark sustains 100% FSR (Task) across nearly all mixing ratios down to 0.4:0.6 donor weight, degrading only mildly at extreme ratios (99% at 0.4:0.6, 89% at 0.3:0.7, 11% at 0.2:0.8, 2% at 0.1:0.9 for Mtask; Mtie stays at 100% across all ratios tested), while Chain&Hash collapses fast (down to 0% by 0.6:0.4) and IF-SFT degrades more gradually (down to 0% by 0.3:0.7). Incremental fine-tuning (Table 5, Mistral-7B-v0.3): FSR decays from 1.00/1.00 (before) to 0.87/0.82 (Light, 1k steps), 0.61/0.55 (Moderate, 5k steps), 0.23/0.18 (Heavy, 20k steps) for FSRprb/FSRrouge respectively -- the fingerprint is not robust to sustained fine-tuning. Ablations: uncertainty-driven key selection substantially outperforms random selection on non-fingerprinted models (Table 6: P(v|k)=0.662 vs 0.091 on Qwen2.5-7B unlearned-baseline check, ROUGE-L 0.432 vs 0.069; on LLaMA3-8B, 0.384 vs 0.084 and 0.269 vs 0.074), which the paper argues widens the pre/post-unlearning margin and reduces false triggers. Larger N strengthens forgetting (Table 7: at N=100, P(v|k) about 1.237e-17 and ROUGE-L=0, harmlessness 0.631; at N=200, P(v|k) about 2.63e-43, ROUGE-L=0, harmlessness drops to 0.593) -- authors select N=100 as the reported trade-off point.
core_contribution: ForgetMark, a stealthy LLM fingerprinting/watermarking method for ownership verification that encodes provenance via targeted machine unlearning instead of a trigger-response backdoor. It (1) builds a compact key-value set by prompting an assistant model (GPT-4) for candidate 'marginalized knowledge' queries, sampling M=3 continuations from the target model per key, and ranking candidates by predictive entropy (Eq. 1) to keep the N lowest-uncertainty (highest-confidence) key-value pairs; (2) trains LoRA adapters with a signed likelihood objective (Eq. 2) that ascends NLL on the fingerprint set F (suppressing P(v|k)) while descending NLL on a retention set Dretain (Alpaca, ratio |Dretain|:|F|=9:1) to preserve general capability; (3) verifies ownership under black-/gray-box access with a Fingerprint Success Rate (FSR, Eq. 3) that flags a success per key if either the suspect model's likelihood on the preset value PMS(v|k) < 10^-3 or ROUGE-L between the suspect's actual output and the preset value < 10^-3.
threat_model: Model owner wants to prove ownership/lineage of a model that may have been illicitly exfiltrated, redistributed, fine-tuned, or merged with another model. Verification is done under black-box access (query in/response out, using ROUGE-L on generated text) or gray-box access (token-level likelihoods available, using PMS(v|k)). The paper explicitly frames prior invasive/backdoor fingerprinting as adversarially fragile because (a) high-perplexity/low-frequency trigger tokens are caught by perplexity filters, (b) fixed trigger-response pairs are exposed by heuristic detectors such as Token Forcing, and (c) fixed triggers can spuriously activate on benign inputs, degrading usability and revealing the fingerprint. ForgetMark's threat model is thus adversary-as-detector-and-derivative-creator: an attacker who filters suspicious prompts, probes for fixed responses, merges the model with another (donor) model, or incrementally fine-tunes it to try to erase or evade the fingerprint.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitation: the unlearning-based fingerprint is only partially robust to sustained incremental fine-tuning -- FSR drops to 0.23/0.18 after 20k steps (Heavy budget) on UltraChat, and the authors note the forgetting trace 'gradually recovers' as fine-tuning accumulates, calling this 'inherent to knowledge unlearning' and leaving anti-recovery mechanisms (periodic refresh, anti-recovery regularizers, interleaved retention) to future work. They also flag as an open question whether fingerprints embedded by ForgetMark transfer across homologous/derivative models, unaddressed in this paper. Additional limitations evident from the text but not foregrounded by the authors: harmlessness/utility cost scales with fingerprint size N (0.631 at N=100 vs 0.593 at N=200, a ~4pt drop on the 18-task average), fingerprint construction depends on an external assistant model (GPT-4) and light manual screening for safety, evaluation is limited to 7-8B parameter open models (no larger-scale or closed-weight validation), and the merging-robustness study is reported only for Mistral (Task/TIE strategies), with DARE variants asserted to show 'the same trend' but not tabulated.

datasets_used: ANLI R1-R3, ARC, OpenBookQA, Winogrande, LogiQA, SciQ, BoolQ, CB, RTE, WiC, WSC, CoPA, MultiRC, LAMBADA-OpenAI, LAMBADA-Standard (18-task zero-shot harmlessness/utility evaluation suite)
quantitative_results: FSR on fingerprinted models: ForgetMark 100%/100% (FSRprb/FSRrouge) vs IF-SFT 100% vs Chain&Hash 90% (Table 1)
Mean zero-shot accuracy over 18 tasks on Qwen2.5-7B: Before 0.640, IF-SFT 0.636, Chain&Hash 0.645, ForgetMark 0.631 (Table 2)
Token Forcing detection rate: IF-SFT 100%, Chain&Hash 50%, ForgetMark 0% (Table 3)

Model merging FSR (Mistral, Task strategy) across mixing ratios 0.9:0.1 down to 0.1:0.9: ForgetMark stays 100% until 0.4:0.6, then 99%/89%/11%/2%; Chain&Hash falls from 90% to 0% by 0.6:0.4; IF-SFT falls from 100% to 0% by 0.3:0.7 (Table 4)
Incremental fine-tuning FSR on Mistral-7B-v0.3: before 1.00/1.00, Light(1k steps) 0.87/0.82, Moderate(5k steps) 0.61/0.55, Heavy(20k steps) 0.23/0.18 (FSRprb/FSRrouge) (Table 5)
Uncertainty-driven vs random key selection (Qwen2.5-7B non-unlearned): P(v|k) 0.662 vs 0.091, ROUGE-L 0.432 vs 0.069; (LLaMA3-8B) P(v|k) 0.384 vs 0.084, ROUGE-L 0.269 vs 0.074 (Table 6)

Effect of key-set size N on Qwen2.5-7B: N=25 harmlessness 0.640, P(v|k) 1.338e-05, ROUGE-L 0.0057; N=50 harmlessness 0.634, P(v|k) 3.295e-12, ROUGE-L 0.0069; N=100 harmlessness 0.631, P(v|k) 1.237e-17, ROUGE-L 0; N=200 harmlessness 0.593, P(v|k) 2.6297e-43, ROUGE-L 0 (Table 7)
baselines_compared: IF-SFT (Instructional Fingerprinting, Xu et al. 2024, trigger-response backdoor using low-probability triggers)
Chain&Hash (Russinovich & Salem 2024, hashes query-output pairs as trigger-response fingerprint)
